Result: Omni-view and depth inputs improve scene transfer and navigation performance; distillation enhances single-view monocular policy performance compared to action imitation only; real-world experiments validate effectiveness.

Conclusion: Proposed distillation method effectively addresses mobile robot vision policy limitations, improving performance while maintaining practicality and cost-effectiveness.

Abstract: Vision-based policies are widely applied in robotics for tasks such as manipulation and locomotion. On lightweight mobile robots, however, they face a trilemma of limited scene transferability, restricted onboard computation resources, and sensor hardware cost. To address these issues, we propose a knowledge distillation approach that transfers knowledge from an information-rich, appearance invariant omniview depth policy to a lightweight monocular policy. The key idea is to train the student not only to mimic the expert actions but also to align with the latent embeddings of the omni view depth teacher. Experiments demonstrate that omni-view and depth inputs improve the scene transfer and navigation performance, and that the proposed distillation method enhances the performance of a singleview monocular policy, compared with policies solely imitating actions. Real world experiments further validate the effectiveness and practicality of our approach. Code will be released publicly.

</details>


### [355] [Swim2Real: VLM-Guided System Identification for Sim-to-Real Transfer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.20827)
*Kevin Qiu,Kyle Walker,Mike Y. Michelis,Marek Cygan,Josie Hughes*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Swim2Real uses vision-language model feedback to calibrate robotic fish simulators from videos, achieving better sim-to-real transfer than traditional methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Calibrating soft aquatic robots is challenging due to nonlinear fluid-structure coupling, persistent sim-to-real gaps from simplified fluid models, and difficulty reproducing controlled aquatic experiments. Prior methods required manually tailored stages to handle this complexity.

Method: A pipeline that calibrates 16-parameter robotic fish simulator using VLM feedback from swimming videos. The VLM compares simulated and real videos to propose parameter updates, with backtracking line search to validate step sizes (tripling accept rate from 14% to 42%).

Result: Calibrates all 16 parameters simultaneously with lowest velocity error (MAE = 7.4 mm/s, 43% lower than next-best). Zero outlier seeds across five runs. Motor commands transfer to physical fish at 50Hz. Downstream RL policies swim 12% farther than BayesOpt-calibrated and 90% farther than CMA-ES.

Conclusion: VLM-guided calibration can close sim-to-real gap for aquatic robots directly from video, enabling zero-shot RL transfer without manual system identification, advancing automated simulator tuning for underwater robotics.

Abstract: We present Swim2Real, a pipeline that calibrates a 16-parameter robotic fish simulator from swimming videos using vision-language model (VLM) feedback, requiring no hand-designed search stages. Calibrating soft aquatic robots is particularly challenging because nonlinear fluid-structure coupling makes the parameter landscape chaotic, simplified fluid models introduce a persistent sim-to-real gap, and controlled aquatic experiments are difficult to reproduce. Prior work on this platform required three manually tailored stages to handle this complexity. The VLM compares simulated and real videos and proposes parameter updates. A backtracking line search then validates each step size, tripling the accept rate from 14% to 42% by recovering proposals where the direction is correct but the magnitude is too large. Swim2Real calibrates all 16 parameters simultaneously, most closely matching real fish velocities across all motor frequencies (MAE = 7.4 mm/s, 43% lower than the next-best method), with zero outlier seeds across five runs. Motor commands from the trained policy transfer to the physical fish at 50 Hz, completing the pipeline from swimming video to real-world deployment. Downstream RL policies swim 12% farther than those from BayesOpt-calibrated simulators and 90% farther than CMA-ES. These results demonstrate that VLM-guided calibration can close the sim-to-real gap for aquatic robots directly from video, enabling zero-shot RL transfer to physical swimmers without manual system identification, a step toward automated, general-purpose simulator tuning for underwater robotics.

</details>


### [356] [Characterizing the onset and offset of motor imagery during passive arm movements induced by an upper-body exoskeleton](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.20885)
*Kanishka Mitra,Frigyes Samuel Racz,Satyam Kumar,Ashish D. Deshpande,José del R. Millán*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Non-invasive BMI detects motor imagery onset/offset during passive exoskeleton movements, enabling natural control of rehabilitation robots despite noise and motion artifacts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Combining robotics and BMIs for motor rehabilitation has clinical potential, but detecting motor intentions during passive exoskeleton movements with non-invasive BMIs is challenging due to noise and motion artifacts.

Method: Ten participants performed kinesthetic motor imagery of right arm movements while attached to an exoskeleton, cued by LEDs for a reaching task. EEG signals were used to build decoders detecting transitions between rest/MI onset and MI maintenance/offset.

Result: Offline decoder achieved 60.7% onset accuracy and 66.6% offset accuracy. Pseudo-online evaluation replicated performance, suggesting reliable future online exoskeleton control. Participants produced quality sensorimotor rhythms despite noise and passive movements.

Conclusion: Motor imagery onset/offset can be detected during passive exoskeleton movements using non-invasive BMI, enabling natural control of assistive devices and opening new possibilities for BMI-controlled rehabilitation robotics.

Abstract: Two distinct technologies have gained attention lately due to their prospects for motor rehabilitation: robotics and brain-machine interfaces (BMIs). Harnessing their combined efforts is a largely uncharted and promising direction that has immense clinical potential. However, a significant challenge is whether motor intentions from the user can be accurately detected using non-invasive BMIs in the presence of instrumental noise and passive movements induced by the rehabilitation exoskeleton. As an alternative to the straightforward continuous control approach, this study instead aims to characterize the onset and offset of motor imagery during passive arm movements induced by an upper-body exoskeleton to allow for the natural control (initiation and termination) of functional movements. Ten participants were recruited to perform kinesthetic motor imagery (MI) of the right arm while attached to the robot, simultaneously cued with LEDs indicating the initiation and termination of a goal-oriented reaching task. Using electroencephalogram signals, we built a decoder to detect the transition between i) rest and beginning MI and ii) maintaining and ending MI. Offline decoder evaluation achieved group average onset accuracy of 60.7% and 66.6% for offset accuracy, revealing that the start and stop of MI could be identified while attached to the robot. Furthermore, pseudo-online evaluation could replicate this performance, forecasting reliable online exoskeleton control in the future. Our approach showed that participants could produce quality and reliable sensorimotor rhythms regardless of noise or passive arm movements induced by wearing the exoskeleton, which opens new possibilities for BMI control of assistive devices.

</details>


### [357] [Implementing Robust M-Estimators with Certifiable Factor Graph Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.20932)
*Zhexin Xu,Hanna Jiamei Zhang,Helena Calatrava,Pau Closas,David M. Rosen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Adaptively reweighted M-estimation with certifiably correct solvers for inner WLS problems using fast local optimization over smooth manifolds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Parameter estimation in robotics/vision faces challenges from outlier contamination and nonconvex optimization landscapes. M-estimation handles outliers but creates severely nonconvex problems. Adaptive reweighting enables M-estimation via WLS subproblems, but solving these inner problems remains challenging due to nonconvex parameter spaces like rotations and poses.

Method: Implement adaptively reweighted M-estimators with certifiably correct solvers for inner WLS subproblems using fast local optimization over smooth manifolds. Exploit recent work on certifiable factor graph optimization to provide global optimality certificates for inner WLS problems while integrating into existing factor graph-based software libraries.

Result: Experimental evaluation on pose-graph optimization and landmark SLAM tasks shows the approach provides higher-quality estimates than alternative local search-based methods while scaling tractably to realistic problem sizes.

Conclusion: The proposed adaptively reweighted certifiable estimation approach effectively combines robust outlier handling with certifiably correct optimization for challenging robotics parameter estimation problems, outperforming existing methods while maintaining practical scalability.

Abstract: Parameter estimation in robotics and computer vision faces formidable challenges from both outlier contamination and nonconvex optimization landscapes. While M-estimation addresses the problem of outliers through robust loss functions, it creates severely nonconvex problems that are difficult to solve globally. Adaptive reweighting schemes provide one particularly appealing strategy for implementing M-estimation in practice: these methods solve a sequence of simpler weighted least squares (WLS) subproblems, enabling both the use of standard least squares solvers and the recovery of higher-quality estimates than simple local search. However, adaptive reweighting still crucially relies upon solving the inner WLS problems effectively, a task that remains challenging in many robotics applications due to the intrinsic nonconvexity of many common parameter spaces (e.g. rotations and poses).
  In this paper, we show how one can easily implement adaptively reweighted M-estimators with certifiably correct solvers for the inner WLS subproblems using only fast local optimization over smooth manifolds. Our approach exploits recent work on certifiable factor graph optimization to provide global optimality certificates for the inner WLS subproblems while seamlessly integrating into existing factor graph-based software libraries and workflows. Experimental evaluation on pose-graph optimization and landmark SLAM tasks demonstrates that our adaptively reweighted certifiable estimation approach provides higher-quality estimates than alternative local search-based methods, while scaling tractably to realistic problem sizes.

</details>


### [358] [Geometrically Plausible Object Pose Refinement using Differentiable Simulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.20992)
*Anil Zeybek,Rhys Newbury,Snehal Dikhale,Nawid Jamali,Soshi Iba,Akansel Cosgun*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Multi-modal pose refinement combining physics simulation, rendering, and visuo-tactile sensing to optimize object poses for both spatial accuracy and physical consistency in dexterous manipulation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current object pose estimation methods often generate geometrically infeasible poses that intersect with robotic hands or aren't properly supported, which is problematic for dexterous manipulation tasks.

Method: Proposes a multi-modal pose refinement approach that combines differentiable physics simulation, differentiable rendering, and visuo-tactile sensing to optimize object poses for spatial accuracy and physical consistency.

Result: Reduces intersection volume error by 73% with accurate initial estimates and over 87% under high initial uncertainty, outperforming ICP-based baselines while also reducing translation and orientation errors.

Conclusion: Achieving physically-grounded pose estimation while remaining faithful to multi-modal sensor inputs is critical for robust in-hand manipulation.

Abstract: State-of-the-art object pose estimation methods are prone to generating geometrically infeasible pose hypotheses. This problem is prevalent in dexterous manipulation, where estimated poses often intersect with the robotic hand or are not lying on a support surface. We propose a multi-modal pose refinement approach that combines differentiable physics simulation, differentiable rendering and visuo-tactile sensing to optimize object poses for both spatial accuracy and physical consistency. Simulated experiments show that our approach reduces the intersection volume error between the object and robotic hand by 73\% when the initial estimate is accurate and by over 87\% under high initial uncertainty, significantly outperforming standard ICP-based baselines. Furthermore, the improvement in geometric plausibility is accompanied by a concurrent reduction in translation and orientation errors. Achieving pose estimation that is grounded in physical reality while remaining faithful to multi-modal sensor inputs is a critical step toward robust in-hand manipulation.

</details>


### [359] [Dreaming the Unseen: World Model-regularized Diffusion Policy for Out-of-Distribution Robustness](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.21017)
*Ziou Hu,Xiangtong Yao,Yuan Meng,Zhenshan Bing,Alois Knoll*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: DDP integrates diffusion world model into policy training to handle severe OOD disturbances by detecting real-imagination discrepancies and switching to internal imagination during anomalies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion policies are vulnerable to catastrophic failures under severe out-of-distribution disturbances like unexpected object displacements or visual corruptions, limiting their real-world robustness.

Method: Dream Diffusion Policy (DDP) deeply integrates a diffusion world model into policy training via shared 3D visual encoder, enabling co-optimization for robust state prediction. During OOD anomalies, it detects real-imagination discrepancies, abandons corrupted visual stream, and relies on internal imagination (autoregressively forecasted latent dynamics) to bypass disruptions.

Result: DDP achieves 73.8% OOD success rate on MetaWorld (vs. 23.9% baseline) and 83.3% success rate under severe real-world spatial shifts (vs. 3.3% baseline). In stress test, maintains 76.7% real-world success rate using only open-loop imagination post-initialization.

Conclusion: DDP demonstrates exceptional resilience to OOD disturbances by integrating predictive imagination into diffusion policies, enabling safe bypass of disruptions and smooth realignment with physical reality through internal world model forecasting.

Abstract: Diffusion policies excel at visuomotor control but often fail catastrophically under severe out-of-distribution (OOD) disturbances, such as unexpected object displacements or visual corruptions. To address this vulnerability, we introduce the Dream Diffusion Policy (DDP), a framework that deeply integrates a diffusion world model into the policy's training objective via a shared 3D visual encoder. This co-optimization endows the policy with robust state-prediction capabilities. When encountering sudden OOD anomalies during inference, DDP detects the real-imagination discrepancy and actively abandons the corrupted visual stream. Instead, it relies on its internal "imagination" (autoregressively forecasted latent dynamics) to safely bypass the disruption, generating imagined trajectories before smoothly realigning with physical reality. Extensive evaluations demonstrate DDP's exceptional resilience. Notably, DDP achieves a 73.8% OOD success rate on MetaWorld (vs. 23.9% without predictive imagination) and an 83.3% success rate under severe real-world spatial shifts (vs. 3.3% without predictive imagination). Furthermore, as a stress test, DDP maintains a 76.7% real-world success rate even when relying entirely on open-loop imagination post-initialization.

</details>


### [360] [Cortical Policy: A Dual-Stream View Transformer for Robotic Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.21051)
*Xuening Zhang,Qi Lv,Xiang Deng,Miao Zhang,Xingbo Liu,Liqiang Nie*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Cortical Policy is a dual-stream view transformer for robotic manipulation that combines static-view spatial reasoning with dynamic-view adaptive adjustment, outperforming SOTA methods on benchmarks and real-world tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing view transformers for robotic manipulation use static, view-specific representations that lack adequate 3D spatial reasoning and dynamic adaptation capabilities, inspired by how the human brain integrates static and dynamic visual information.

Method: A dual-stream architecture with: 1) Static-view stream using pretrained 3D foundation model to extract geometrically consistent keypoints for spatial understanding; 2) Dynamic-view stream using position-aware pretrained egocentric gaze estimation model (mimicking human cortical dorsal pathway); 3) Integration of complementary representations for final action prediction under language conditions.

Result: Outperforms state-of-the-art baselines substantially on RLBench, challenging COLOSSEUM benchmark, and real-world tasks, validating superiority of dual-stream design for visuomotor control.

Conclusion: The cortex-inspired dual-stream framework offers a fresh perspective for robotic manipulation with potential for broader application in vision-based robot control, demonstrating that combining static spatial reasoning with dynamic adaptation improves performance on spatially-complex and dynamically-changing tasks.

Abstract: View transformers process multi-view observations to predict actions and have shown impressive performance in robotic manipulation. Existing methods typically extract static visual representations in a view-specific manner, leading to inadequate 3D spatial reasoning ability and a lack of dynamic adaptation. Taking inspiration from how the human brain integrates static and dynamic views to address these challenges, we propose Cortical Policy, a novel dual-stream view transformer for robotic manipulation that jointly reasons from static-view and dynamic-view streams. The static-view stream enhances spatial understanding by aligning features of geometrically consistent keypoints extracted from a pretrained 3D foundation model. The dynamic-view stream achieves adaptive adjustment through position-aware pretraining of an egocentric gaze estimation model, computationally replicating the human cortical dorsal pathway. Subsequently, the complementary view representations of both streams are integrated to determine the final actions, enabling the model to handle spatially-complex and dynamically-changing tasks under language conditions. Empirical evaluations on RLBench, the challenging COLOSSEUM benchmark, and real-world tasks demonstrate that Cortical Policy outperforms state-of-the-art baselines substantially, validating the superiority of dual-stream design for visuomotor control. Our cortex-inspired framework offers a fresh perspective for robotic manipulation and holds potential for broader application in vision-based robot control.

</details>


### [361] [CounterScene: Counterfactual Causal Reasoning in Generative World Models for Safety-Critical Closed-Loop Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.21104)
*Bowen Jing,Ruiyang Hao,Weitao Zhou,Haibao Yu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: CounterScene is a framework for generating safety-critical driving scenarios using structured counterfactual reasoning in BEV world models, achieving better adversarial effectiveness and realism than existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for generating safety-critical driving scenarios rely on heuristic adversarial agent selection and unstructured perturbations, creating a realism-adversarial trade-off. They lack explicit modeling of interaction dependencies needed to understand why dangerous interactions arise.

Method: CounterScene uses causal adversarial agent identification to find critical agents and classify conflict types, then builds a conflict-aware interactive world model with causal interaction graphs to model inter-agent dependencies. Stage-adaptive counterfactual guidance performs minimal interventions on identified agents, removing safety margins while allowing risk to emerge through natural interaction propagation.

Result: On nuScenes, CounterScene achieves strongest adversarial effectiveness while maintaining superior trajectory realism, improving long-horizon collision rate from 12.3% to 22.7% over strongest baselines with better realism (ADE 1.88 vs. 2.09). Advantages widen over longer rollouts, and the method generalizes zero-shot to nuPlan with state-of-the-art realism.

Conclusion: CounterScene demonstrates that structured counterfactual reasoning in generative world models can overcome the realism-adversarial trade-off in safety-critical scenario generation by explicitly modeling interaction dependencies and performing minimal, targeted interventions.

Abstract: Generating safety-critical driving scenarios requires understanding why dangerous interactions arise, rather than merely forcing collisions. However, existing methods rely on heuristic adversarial agent selection and unstructured perturbations, lacking explicit modeling of interaction dependencies and thus exhibiting a realism--adversarial trade-off. We present CounterScene, a framework that endows closed-loop generative BEV world models with structured counterfactual reasoning for safety-critical scenario generation. Given a safe scene, CounterScene asks: what if the causally critical agent had behaved differently? To answer this, we introduce causal adversarial agent identification to identify the critical agent and classify conflict types, and develop a conflict-aware interactive world model in which a causal interaction graph is used to explicitly model dynamic inter-agent dependencies. Building on this structure, stage-adaptive counterfactual guidance performs minimal interventions on the identified agent, removing its spatial and temporal safety margins while allowing risk to emerge through natural interaction propagation. Extensive experiments on nuScenes demonstrate that CounterScene achieves the strongest adversarial effectiveness while maintaining superior trajectory realism across all horizons, improving long-horizon collision rate from 12.3% to 22.7% over the strongest baseline with better realism (ADE 1.88 vs.2.09). Notably, this advantage further widens over longer rollouts, and CounterScene generalizes zero-shot to nuPlan with state-of-the-art realism.

</details>


### [362] [VisFly-Lab: Unified Differentiable Framework for First-Order Reinforcement Learning of Quadrotor Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.21123)
*Fanxing Li,Fangyu Sun,Tianbao Zhang,Shuyu Wu,Dexin Zuo,yufei Yan,Wenxian Yu,Danping Zou*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A unified differentiable simulation framework for multi-task quadrotor control with ABPT algorithm addressing training bottlenecks, showing improved robustness and real-world transferability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: First-order reinforcement learning with differentiable simulation shows promise for quadrotor control, but progress is fragmented across task-specific settings, lacking systematic development and evaluation frameworks.

Method: Proposes a unified differentiable framework for multi-task quadrotor control with four representative tasks (hovering, tracking, landing, racing), and introduces Amended Backpropagation Through Time (ABPT) algorithm combining differentiable rollout optimization, value-based auxiliary objective, and visited-state initialization to address training bottlenecks.

Result: ABPT yields clearest gains in tasks with partially non-differentiable rewards while remaining competitive in fully differentiable settings. Real-world deployments show initial transferability of policies learned in the framework beyond simulation.

Conclusion: The proposed unified differentiable framework with ABPT algorithm provides a systematic approach for multi-task quadrotor control, addressing practical training bottlenecks and demonstrating promising real-world transferability.

Abstract: First-order reinforcement learning with differentiable simulation is promising for quadrotor control, but practical progress remains fragmented across task-specific settings. To support more systematic development and evaluation, we present a unified differentiable framework for multi-task quadrotor control. The framework is wrapped, extensible, and equipped with deployment-oriented dynamics, providing a common interface across four representative tasks: hovering, tracking, landing, and racing. We also present the suite of first-order learning algorithms, where we identify two practical bottlenecks of standard first-order training: limited state coverage caused by horizon initialization and gradient bias caused by partially non-differentiable rewards. To address these issues, we propose Amended Backpropagation Through Time (ABPT), which combines differentiable rollout optimization, a value-based auxiliary objective, and visited-state initialization to improve training robustness. Experimental results show that ABPT yields the clearest gains in tasks with partially non-differentiable rewards, while remaining competitive in fully differentiable settings. We further provide proof-of-concept real-world deployments showing initial transferability of policies learned in the proposed framework beyond simulation.

</details>


### [363] [Anatomical Prior-Driven Framework for Autonomous Robotic Cardiac Ultrasound Standard View Acquisition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.21134)
*Zhiyan Cao,Zhengxi Wu,Yiwei Wang,Pei-Hsuan Lin,Li Zhang,Zhen Xie,Huan Zhao,Han Ding*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: AP-driven framework integrates cardiac segmentation with autonomous probe adjustment using SRG-YOLO for anatomical segmentation and RL for probe control, improving both segmentation accuracy and standard view acquisition success.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cardiac ultrasound diagnosis requires standard views but is operator-dependent; existing methods produce anatomically inconsistent results or use simplistic heuristics for probe adjustment.

Method: YOLO-based multi-class segmentation with spatial-relation graph module embeds anatomical priors; extracts quantifiable features fitted to Gaussian distributions; formalizes probe adjustment as RL problem with state from real-time features and reward based on AP matching.

Result: SRG-YOLOv11s improves mAP50 by 11.3% and mIoU by 6.8% on Special Case dataset; RL agent achieves 92.5% success rate in simulation and 86.7% in phantom experiments.

Conclusion: The AP-driven framework effectively integrates anatomical knowledge into both segmentation and probe adjustment, improving consistency and automation in cardiac ultrasound standard view acquisition.

Abstract: Cardiac ultrasound diagnosis is critical for cardiovascular disease assessment, but acquiring standard views remains highly operator-dependent. Existing medical segmentation models often yield anatomically inconsistent results in images with poor textural differentiation between distinct feature classes, while autonomous probe adjustment methods either rely on simplistic heuristic rules or black-box learning. To address these issues, our study proposed an anatomical prior (AP)-driven framework integrating cardiac structure segmentation and autonomous probe adjustment for standard view acquisition. A YOLO-based multi-class segmentation model augmented by a spatial-relation graph (SRG) module is designed to embed AP into the feature pyramid. Quantifiable anatomical features of standard views are extracted. Their priors are fitted to Gaussian distributions to construct probabilistic APs. The probe adjustment process of robotic ultrasound scanning is formalized as a reinforcement learning (RL) problem, with the RL state built from real-time anatomical features and the reward reflecting the AP matching. Experiments validate the efficacy of the framework. The SRG-YOLOv11s improves mAP50 by 11.3% and mIoU by 6.8% on the Special Case dataset, while the RL agent achieves a 92.5% success rate in simulation and 86.7% in phantom experiments.

</details>


### [364] [Dynamic Control Barrier Function Regulation with Vision-Language Models for Safe, Adaptive, and Realtime Visual Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.21142)
*Jeffrey Chen,Rohan Chandra*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: AlphaAdj is a vision-to-control navigation framework that uses RGB input and vision-language models to dynamically adapt CBF safety filter conservativeness in real time, improving efficiency while maintaining safety.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robots in dynamic environments need to balance safety and efficiency, but traditional CBF safety filters use fixed parameters that can be overly conservative in safe scenes or overly permissive near hazards, limiting performance.

Method: Uses egocentric RGB input with a vision-language model to produce real-time risk estimates, maps these to dynamically update CBF parameters that modulate safety constraint enforcement. Addresses VLM latency with geometric speed-aware dynamic caps and staleness-gated fusion policies.

Result: AlphaAdj maintains collision-free navigation while improving efficiency (path length and time to goal) by up to 18.5% compared to fixed settings, and improves robustness and success rate compared to uncapped baselines.

Conclusion: The framework successfully demonstrates that dynamic adaptation of CBF parameters using vision-based risk assessment enables robots to achieve better efficiency-safety trade-offs in real-world navigation scenarios.

Abstract: Robots operating in dynamic, unstructured environments must balance safety and efficiency under potentially limited sensing. While control barrier functions (CBFs) provide principled collision avoidance via safety filtering, their behavior is often governed by fixed parameters that can be overly conservative in benign scenes or overly permissive near hazards. We present AlphaAdj, a vision-to-control navigation framework that uses egocentric RGB input to adapt the conservativeness of a CBF safety filter in real time. A vision-language model(VLM) produces a bounded scalar risk estimate from the current camera view, which we map to dynamically update a CBF parameter that modulates how strongly safety constraints are enforced. To address asynchronous inference and non-trivial VLM latency in practice, we combine a geometric, speed-aware dynamic cap and a staleness-gated fusion policy with lightweight implementation choices that reduce end-to-end inference overhead. We evaluate AlphaAdj across multiple static and dynamic obstacle scenarios in a variety of environments, comparing against fixed-parameter and uncapped ablations. Results show that AlphaAdj maintains collision-free navigation while improving efficiency (in terms of path length and time to goal) by up to 18.5% relative to fixed settings and improving robustness and success rate relative to an uncapped baseline.

</details>


### [365] [Affordance-Guided Enveloping Grasp Demonstration Toward Non-destructive Disassembly of Pinch-Infeasible Mating Parts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.21143)
*Masaki Tsutsumi,Takuya Kiyokawa,Gen Sako,Kensuke Harada*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: AT-based teleoperation method uses physics-simulated grasp templates with quality visualization to teach enveloping grasps for robotic disassembly under visual/geometric constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robotic disassembly of complex mating components often requires multi-fingered enveloping grasps instead of pinch grasps, but visual occlusions and geometric constraints make teaching appropriate grasp motions difficult using only 2D camera feeds.

Method: Proposed affordance-guided teleoperation method pre-generates enveloping grasp candidates via physics simulation, creating Affordance Templates (ATs) visualized with color gradients reflecting grasp quality to augment operator perception.

Result: Simulations demonstrate method's generality across various components. Real-robot experiments validate that AT-based visual augmentation enables operators to effectively select and teach enveloping grasp strategies for real-world disassembly, even under severe visual and geometric constraints.

Conclusion: The AT-based teleoperation method successfully addresses the challenge of teaching enveloping grasps for robotic disassembly by providing visual augmentation of pre-generated grasp candidates, enabling effective operation under challenging conditions.

Abstract: Robotic disassembly of complex mating components often renders pinch grasping infeasible, necessitating multi-fingered enveloping grasps. However, visual occlusions and geometric constraints complicate teaching appropriate grasp motions when relying solely on 2D camera feeds. To address this, we propose an affordance-guided teleoperation method that pre-generates enveloping grasp candidates via physics simulation. These Affordance Templates (ATs) are visualized with a color gradient reflecting grasp quality to augment operator perception. Simulations demonstrate the method's generality across various components. Real-robot experiments validate that AT-based visual augmentation enables operators to effectively select and teach enveloping grasp strategies for real-world disassembly, even under severe visual and geometric constraints.

</details>


### [366] [Architecture for Multi-Unmanned Aerial Vehicles based Autonomous Precision Agriculture Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.21183)
*Ebasa Temesgen,Nathnael Minyelshowa,Lebsework Negash*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proposes an architectural framework for multi-UAV systems in precision agriculture that enables autonomous end-to-end operations with minimal physical intervention.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The rapid increase in UAV use for precision agriculture requires structured frameworks to apply various algorithms efficiently, with minimal physical intervention and coordinated multi-UAV operations.

Method: Defines UAV tasks in precision agriculture and models them into an architectural framework that includes image processing, path planning, communication, data acquisition, and field mapping components, considering limitations of multi-UAV applications.

Result: Simulation and field tests demonstrate the architecture offers fault-tolerance, robustness, developer and user-friendliness advantages, providing an efficient autonomous end-to-end solution from mission planning to data processing.

Conclusion: The proposed architecture successfully enables comprehensive UAV deployment in precision agriculture with coordinated multi-UAV operations, addressing practical limitations while providing an efficient, autonomous framework.

Abstract: The use of unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) in precision agriculture has seen a huge increase recently. As such, systems that aim to apply various algorithms on the field need a structured framework of abstractions. This paper defines the various tasks of the UAVs in precision agriculture and model them into an architectural framework. The presented architecture is built on the context that there will be minimal physical intervention to do the tasks defined with multiple coordinated and cooperative UAVs. Various tasks such as image processing, path planning, communication, data acquisition, and field mapping are employed in the architecture to provide an efficient system. Besides, different limitation for applying Multi-UAVs in precision agriculture has been considered in designing the architecture. The architecture provides an autonomous end-to-end solution, starting from mission planning, data acquisition and image processing framework that is highly efficient and can enable farmers to comprehensively deploy UAVs onto their lands. Simulation and field tests shows that the architecture offers a number of advantages that include fault-tolerance, robustness, developer and user-friendliness.

</details>


### [367] [GAPG: Geometry Aware Push-Grasping Synergy for Goal-Oriented Manipulation in Clutter](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.21195)
*Lijingze Xiao,Jinhong Du,Yang Cong,Supeng Diao,Yu Ren*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Geometry-aware push-grasp synergy framework that uses point cloud data to jointly evaluate grasp feasibility and push actions for safer, more efficient manipulation in cluttered environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Single-step grasping is insufficient in cluttered environments with stacked/occluded objects. Previous push-assisted methods lack stability and efficiency because they ignore geometric information crucial for evaluating grasp robustness and ensuring safe/effective pushing actions.

Method: Proposes a geometry-aware push-grasp synergy framework with two modules: 1) Grasp evaluation analyzes geometric relationship between gripper's point cloud and points in closing region to determine grasp feasibility/stability; 2) Push evaluation predicts how pushing actions influence future graspable space, enabling transformation of non-graspable to graspable states.

Result: Extensively tested in simulation and real-world environments. Demonstrates good generalization to real-world scenes and unseen objects. Achieves safer, more efficient, and more reliable manipulation in cluttered settings.

Conclusion: Joint geometric reasoning about both grasping and pushing enables more effective manipulation in cluttered environments, overcoming limitations of previous methods that neglect scene geometry for evaluating grasp robustness and push effectiveness.

Abstract: Grasping target objects is a fundamental skill for robotic manipulation, but in cluttered environments with stacked or occluded objects, a single-step grasp is often insufficient. To address this, previous work has introduced pushing as an auxiliary action to create graspable space. However, these methods often struggle with both stability and efficiency because they neglect the scene's geometric information, which is essential for evaluating grasp robustness and ensuring that pushing actions are safe and effective. To this end, we propose a geometry-aware push-grasp synergy framework that leverages point cloud data to integrate grasp and push evaluation. Specifically, the grasp evaluation module analyzes the geometric relationship between the gripper's point cloud and the points enclosed within its closing region to determine grasp feasibility and stability. Guided by this, the push evaluation module predicts how pushing actions influence future graspable space, enabling the robot to select actions that reliably transform non-graspable states into graspable ones. By jointly reasoning about geometry in both grasping and pushing, our framework achieves safer, more efficient, and more reliable manipulation in cluttered settings. Our method is extensively tested in simulation and real-world environments in various scenarios. Experimental results demonstrate that our model generalizes well to real-world scenes and unseen objects.

</details>


### [368] [Evaluating Factor-Wise Auxiliary Dynamics Supervision for Latent Structure and Robustness in Simulated Humanoid Locomotion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.21268)
*Chayanin Chamachot*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Auxiliary dynamics supervision in DynaMITE fails to create interpretable latent structure or improve robustness in humanoid locomotion; bottleneck compression helps slightly but LSTM baselines perform best.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To evaluate whether factor-wise auxiliary dynamics supervision produces useful latent structure or improved robustness in simulated humanoid locomotion tasks.

Method: DynaMITE (transformer encoder with factored 24-d latent trained by per-factor auxiliary losses during PPO) compared against LSTM, Transformer, and MLP baselines on Unitree G1 humanoid across four Isaac Lab tasks. Used 2x2 factorial ablation to isolate contributions of tanh bottleneck and auxiliary losses.

Result: Supervised latent shows no decodable factor structure (probe R^2 ~ 0, disentanglement metrics near zero). Auxiliary losses show no measurable effect on reward (+0.03, p > 0.6). Bottleneck shows small advantage (+0.16 ID, +0.10 OOD). LSTM achieves best nominal reward on all tasks. DynaMITE degrades less under stress (2.3% vs 16.7%), but this is due to bottleneck compression, not auxiliary supervision.

Conclusion: Auxiliary dynamics supervision doesn't produce interpretable estimators or improve reward/robustness beyond bottleneck alone. Recurrent baselines (LSTM) remain stronger for nominal performance in locomotion tasks.

Abstract: We evaluate whether factor-wise auxiliary dynamics supervision produces useful latent structure or improved robustness in simulated humanoid locomotion. DynaMITE -- a transformer encoder with a factored 24-d latent trained by per-factor auxiliary losses during proximal policy optimization (PPO) -- is compared against Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM), plain Transformer, and Multilayer Perceptron (MLP) baselines on a Unitree G1 humanoid across four Isaac Lab tasks. The supervised latent shows no evidence of decodable or functionally separable factor structure: probe R^2 ~ 0 for all five dynamics factors, clamping any subspace changes reward by < 0.05, and standard disentanglement metrics (MIG, DCI, SAP) are near zero. An unsupervised LSTM hidden state achieves higher probe R^2 (up to 0.10). A 2x2 factorial ablation (n = 10 seeds) isolates the contributions of the tanh bottleneck and auxiliary losses: the auxiliary losses show no measurable effect on either in-distribution (ID) reward (+0.03, p = 0.732) or severe out-of-distribution (OOD) reward (+0.03, p = 0.669), while the bottleneck shows a small, consistent advantage in both regimes (ID: +0.16, p = 0.207; OOD: +0.10, p = 0.208). The bottleneck advantage persists under severe combined perturbation but does not amplify, indicating a training-time representation benefit rather than a robustness mechanism. LSTM achieves the best nominal reward on all four tasks (p < 0.03); DynaMITE degrades less under combined-shift stress (2.3% vs. 16.7%), but this difference is attributable to the bottleneck compression, not the auxiliary supervision. For locomotion practitioners: auxiliary dynamics supervision does not produce an interpretable estimator and does not measurably improve reward or robustness beyond what the bottleneck alone provides; recurrent baselines remain the stronger choice for nominal performance.

</details>


### [369] [DyGeoVLN: Infusing Dynamic Geometry Foundation Model into Vision-Language Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.21269)
*Xiangchen Liu,Hanghan Zheng,Jeil Jeong,Minsung Yoon,Lin Zhao,Zhide Zhong,Haoang Li,Sung-Eui Yoon*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: DyGeoVLN: A dynamic geometry-aware vision-language navigation framework that incorporates 3D spatial reasoning and adaptive token pruning for efficient navigation in real-world dynamic environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VLN approaches rely on static scene assumptions and struggle to generalize to dynamic, real-world scenarios where environments change and move.

Method: Infuses a dynamic geometry foundation model into VLN through cross-branch feature fusion for explicit 3D spatial representation, plus a novel pose-free adaptive-resolution token-pruning strategy to compress historical information in long-horizon navigation.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on multiple benchmarks and exhibits strong robustness in real-world environments while reducing inference cost through token pruning.

Conclusion: DyGeoVLN effectively addresses the limitations of static-scene VLN approaches by enabling dynamic geometry-aware navigation with efficient token management for real-world applications.

Abstract: Vision-language Navigation (VLN) requires an agent to understand visual observations and language instructions to navigate in unseen environments. Most existing approaches rely on static scene assumptions and struggle to generalize in dynamic, real-world scenarios. To address this challenge, we propose DyGeoVLN, a dynamic geometry-aware VLN framework. Our method infuses a dynamic geometry foundation model into the VLN framework through cross-branch feature fusion to enable explicit 3D spatial representation and visual-semantic reasoning. To efficiently compress historical token information in long-horizon, dynamic navigation, we further introduce a novel pose-free and adaptive-resolution token-pruning strategy. This strategy can remove spatio-temporal redundant tokens to reduce inference cost. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance on multiple benchmarks and exhibits strong robustness in real-world environments.

</details>


### [370] [Bayesian Active Object Recognition and 6D Pose Estimation from Multimodal Contact Sensing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.21410)
*Haodong Zheng,Gabriele M. Caddeo,Andrei C. Jalba,Wijnand A. IJsselsteijn,Lorenzo Natale,Raymond H. Cuijpers*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Active tactile exploration framework for joint object recognition and 6D pose estimation using Bayesian inference with wrist force/torque and GelSight tactile sensing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve object recognition and pose estimation by combining tactile and free-space information through active exploration, reducing ambiguity and improving robustness in cluttered environments.

Method: Integrates force/torque sensing, GelSight tactile sensing, and free-space constraints in Bayesian inference framework with customized particle filter; uses active exploration planning with tactile servoing for data collection.

Result: Substantial improvement in recognition and pose estimation accuracy/stability with fewer action cycles compared to force/torque-only baselines, validated on 11 YCB objects.

Conclusion: Combining tactile and free-space information through active Bayesian inference significantly enhances joint object recognition and pose estimation performance.

Abstract: We present an active tactile exploration framework for joint object recognition and 6D pose estimation. The proposed method integrates wrist force/torque sensing, GelSight tactile sensing, and free-space constraints within a Bayesian inference framework that maintains a belief over object class and pose during active tactile exploration. By combining contact and non-contact evidence, the framework reduces ambiguity and improves robustness in the joint class-pose estimation problem. To enable efficient inference in the large hypothesis space, we employ a customized particle filter that progressively samples particles based on new observations. The inferred belief is further used to guide active exploration by selecting informative next touches under reachability constraints. For effective data collection, a motion planning and control framework is developed to plan and execute feasible paths for tactile exploration, handle unexpected contacts and GelSight-surface alignment with tactile servoing. We evaluate the framework in simulation and on a Franka Panda robot using 11 YCB objects. Results show that incorporating tactile and free-space information substantially improves recognition and pose estimation accuracy and stability, while reducing the number of action cycles compared with force/torque-only baselines. Code, dataset, and supplementary material will be made available online.

</details>


### [371] [HyReach: Vision-Guided Hybrid Manipulator Reaching in Unseen Cluttered Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.21421)
*Shivani Kamtikar,Kendall Koe,Justin Wasserman,Samhita Marri,Benjamin Walt,Naveen Kumar Uppalapati,Girish Krishnan,Girish Chowdhary*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Real-time hybrid rigid-soft continuum manipulator system for robust object reaching in unstructured, cluttered environments using vision-based perception and learning-based control.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robotic systems need to operate in unstructured, cluttered, and previously unseen environments, requiring manipulators that combine compliance, adaptability, and precise control.

Method: Integrates vision-based perception and 3D scene reconstruction with shape-aware motion planning. Uses learning-based controller to drive hybrid arm to target poses, leveraging soft segment flexibility while maintaining rigid segment precision.

Result: System operates without environment-specific retraining, enabling direct generalization to new scenes. Real-world experiments show consistent reaching performance with errors below 2 cm across diverse cluttered setups.

Conclusion: Hybrid rigid-soft continuum manipulators show potential for adaptive and reliable operation in unstructured environments, combining compliance with precision control.

Abstract: As robotic systems increasingly operate in unstructured, cluttered, and previously unseen environments, there is a growing need for manipulators that combine compliance, adaptability, and precise control. This work presents a real-time hybrid rigid-soft continuum manipulator system designed for robust open-world object reaching in such challenging environments. The system integrates vision-based perception and 3D scene reconstruction with shape-aware motion planning to generate safe trajectories. A learning-based controller drives the hybrid arm to arbitrary target poses, leveraging the flexibility of the soft segment while maintaining the precision of the rigid segment. The system operates without environment-specific retraining, enabling direct generalization to new scenes. Extensive real-world experiments demonstrate consistent reaching performance with errors below 2 cm across diverse cluttered setups, highlighting the potential of hybrid manipulators for adaptive and reliable operation in unstructured environments.

</details>


### [372] [GaussianSSC: Triplane-Guided Directional Gaussian Fields for 3D Semantic Completion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.21487)
*Ruiqi Xian,Jing Liang,He Yin,Xuewei Qi,Dinesh Manocha*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: GaussianSSC is a two-stage semantic scene completion method that integrates Gaussian representations with voxel grids and triplanes to improve monocular occupancy estimation and semantic prediction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To leverage the benefits of Gaussian representations for semantic scene completion without replacing the voxel grid or maintaining separate Gaussian sets, aiming to improve voxel-image alignment and capture surface properties like tangency, scale, and occlusion-aware asymmetry.

Method: Two-stage approach with Gaussian Anchoring for sub-pixel Gaussian-weighted image aggregation over FPN features, conversion of voxel features into per-voxel Gaussian fields, and Gaussian-Triplane Refinement module with local gathering and global aggregation for directional, anisotropic support.

Result: On SemanticKITTI: Stage 1 occupancy improves by +1.0% Recall, +2.0% Precision, +1.8% IoU over SOTA baselines; Stage 2 semantic prediction improves by +1.8% IoU and +0.8% mIoU.

Conclusion: GaussianSSC successfully integrates Gaussian representations with voxel grids and triplanes, achieving state-of-the-art performance in semantic scene completion while preserving the efficiency of triplane representations.

Abstract: We present \emph{GaussianSSC}, a two-stage, grid-native and triplane-guided approach to semantic scene completion (SSC) that injects the benefits of Gaussians without replacing the voxel grid or maintaining a separate Gaussian set. We introduce \emph{Gaussian Anchoring}, a sub-pixel, Gaussian-weighted image aggregation over fused FPN features that tightens voxel--image alignment and improves monocular occupancy estimation. We further convert point-like voxel features into a learned per-voxel Gaussian field and refine triplane features via a triplane-aligned \emph{Gaussian--Triplane Refinement} module that combines \emph{local gathering} (target-centric) and \emph{global aggregation} (source-centric). This directional, anisotropic support captures surface tangency, scale, and occlusion-aware asymmetry while preserving the efficiency of triplane representations. On SemanticKITTI~\cite{behley2019semantickitti}, GaussianSSC improves Stage~1 occupancy by +1.0\% Recall, +2.0\% Precision, and +1.8\% IoU over state-of-the-art baselines, and improves Stage~2 semantic prediction by +1.8\% IoU and +0.8\% mIoU.

</details>


### [373] [A Framework for Closed-Loop Robotic Assembly, Alignment and Self-Recovery of Precision Optical Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.21496)
*Seou Choi,Sachin Vaidya,Caio Silva,Shiekh Zia Uddin,Sajib Biswas Shuvo,Shrish Choudhary,Marin Soljačić*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Robotics framework enables fully autonomous construction, alignment, and maintenance of precision optical systems, demonstrated by building a tabletop laser cavity from scattered components.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Free-space optics remains largely manual despite robotic automation transforming other scientific domains, due to strict spatial/angular tolerances and tightly coupled physical parameters that make generalizable automation challenging.

Method: Integrates hierarchical computer vision systems, optimization routines, and custom-built tools to achieve autonomous optical system construction and alignment.

Result: Successfully demonstrated fully autonomous construction of a tabletop laser cavity from randomly distributed components, performing beam centering, spatial alignment, resonator alignment, mode selection, and self-recovery from disturbances.

Conclusion: Establishes foundation for autonomous optical experiments by achieving closed-loop autonomy for highly sensitive optical systems, with applications across technical domains.

Abstract: Robotic automation has transformed scientific workflows in domains such as chemistry and materials science, yet free-space optics, which is a high precision domain, remains largely manual. Optical systems impose strict spatial and angular tolerances, and their performance is governed by tightly coupled physical parameters, making generalizable automation particularly challenging. In this work, we present a robotics framework for the autonomous construction, alignment, and maintenance of precision optical systems. Our approach integrates hierarchical computer vision systems, optimization routines, and custom-built tools to achieve this functionality. As a representative demonstration, we perform the fully autonomous construction of a tabletop laser cavity from randomly distributed components. The system performs several tasks such as laser beam centering, spatial alignment of multiple beams, resonator alignment, laser mode selection, and self-recovery from induced misalignment and disturbances. By achieving closed-loop autonomy for highly sensitive optical systems, this work establishes a foundation for autonomous optical experiments for applications across technical domains.

</details>


### [374] [SafePilot: A Framework for Assuring LLM-enabled Cyber-Physical Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.21523)
*Weizhe Xu,Mengyu Liu,Fanxin Kong*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SafePilot is a hierarchical neuro-symbolic framework that provides end-to-end assurance for LLM-enabled cyber-physical systems by verifying outputs against formal specifications and using iterative refinement to prevent unsafe hallucinations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs integrated into cyber-physical systems (robotics, industrial automation, autopilot) can produce dangerous "hallucinations" - coherent but factually incorrect outputs that lead to unsafe actions. There's a critical need to assure LLM-enabled CPS safety.

Method: SafePilot uses a hierarchical planner with a discriminator to assess task complexity. For manageable tasks, it uses an LLM-based task planner with built-in verification. For complex tasks, it applies divide-and-conquer: decomposes tasks into sub-tasks, plans each individually, then merges solutions. The LLM planner translates natural language constraints into formal specifications and iteratively verifies/refines outputs until valid or limit reached.

Result: The framework supports LLM-enabled CPS with both attribute-based and temporal constraints. Its effectiveness and adaptability are demonstrated through two illustrative case studies.

Conclusion: SafePilot provides a novel approach to assuring LLM-enabled cyber-physical systems by combining hierarchical planning with formal verification to mitigate hallucination risks and ensure safe operation according to specifications.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs), deep learning architectures with typically over 10 billion parameters, have recently begun to be integrated into various cyber-physical systems (CPS) such as robotics, industrial automation, and autopilot systems. The abstract knowledge and reasoning capabilities of LLMs are employed for tasks like planning and navigation. However, a significant challenge arises from the tendency of LLMs to produce "hallucinations" - outputs that are coherent yet factually incorrect or contextually unsuitable. This characteristic can lead to undesirable or unsafe actions in the CPS. Therefore, our research focuses on assuring the LLM-enabled CPS by enhancing their critical properties. We propose SafePilot, a novel hierarchical neuro-symbolic framework that provides end-to-end assurance for LLM-enabled CPS according to attribute-based and temporal specifications. Given a task and its specification, SafePilot first invokes a hierarchical planner with a discriminator that assesses task complexity. If the task is deemed manageable, it is passed directly to an LLM-based task planner with built-in verification. Otherwise, the hierarchical planner applies a divide-and-conquer strategy, decomposing the task into sub-tasks, each of which is individually planned and later merged into a final solution. The LLM-based task planner translates natural language constraints into formal specifications and verifies the LLM's output against them. If violations are detected, it identifies the flaw, adjusts the prompt accordingly, and re-invokes the LLM. This iterative process continues until a valid plan is produced or a predefined limit is reached. Our framework supports LLM-enabled CPS with both attribute-based and temporal constraints. Its effectiveness and adaptability are demonstrated through two illustrative case studies.

</details>


### [375] [Auction-Based Task Allocation with Energy-Conscientious Trajectory Optimization for AMR Fleets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.21545)
*Jiachen Li,Soovadeep Bakshi,Jian Chu,Shihao Li,Dongmei Chen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Hierarchical two-stage framework for multi-robot task allocation with sequential auction and trajectory optimization achieves 11.8% energy savings over baseline, with performance depending on workspace friction heterogeneity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need efficient multi-robot task allocation and trajectory optimization in asymmetric task spaces with energy constraints, robot faults, and dynamic events like priority arrivals.

Method: Two-stage framework: (1) sequential auction with closed-form bid functions for task allocation, (2) independent optimal control for energy-minimal trajectories with physics-based battery model and collision avoidance refinement. Event-triggered warm-start rescheduling handles faults and dynamic events.

Result: 11.8% average energy savings over nearest-task allocation across 505 scenarios with 2-20 robots and up to 100 tasks. Bid-metric performance is regime-dependent: distance bids outperform in uniform workspaces (3.5% better), while energy bids win in heterogeneous friction environments (2-2.4% better).

Conclusion: Practitioner guidance: use distance bids in near-uniform terrain and energy-aware bids when friction variation is significant. The framework provides efficient rescheduling with under 10 ms latency and handles dynamic events effectively.

Abstract: This paper presents a hierarchical two-stage framework for multi-robot task allocation and trajectory optimization in asymmetric task spaces: (1) a sequential auction allocates tasks using closed-form bid functions, and (2) each robot independently solves an optimal control problem for energy-minimal trajectories with a physics-based battery model, followed by a collision avoidance refinement step using pairwise proximity penalties. Event-triggered warm-start rescheduling with bounded trigger frequency handles robot faults, priority arrivals, and energy deviations. Across 505 scenarios with 2-20 robots and up to 100 tasks on three factory layouts, both energy- and distance-based auction variants achieve 11.8% average energy savings over nearest-task allocation, with rescheduling latency under 10 ms. The central finding is that bid-metric performance is regime-dependent: in uniform workspaces, distance bids outperform energy bids by 3.5% (p < 0.05, Wilcoxon) because a 15.7% closed-form approximation error degrades bid ranking accuracy to 87%; however, when workspace friction heterogeneity is sufficient (r < 0.85 energy-distance correlation), a zone-aware energy bid outperforms distance bids by 2-2.4%. These results provide practitioner guidance: use distance bids in near-uniform terrain and energy-aware bids when friction variation is significant.

</details>


### [376] [Conformal Koopman for Embedded Nonlinear Control with Statistical Robustness: Theory and Real-World Validation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.21580)
*Koki Hirano,Hiroyasu Tsukamoto*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A Koopman-based control framework with conformal prediction provides distribution-free probabilistic safety guarantees for nonlinear systems under modeling uncertainty.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing Koopman-based control methods lack formal safety guarantees under modeling uncertainty, especially when combining conformal prediction with control in open-loop settings without accounting for both forward and inverse modeling errors.

Method: Combines Koopman operator theory with contraction theory and conformal prediction to derive distribution-free probabilistic bounds on state tracking error, establishing a closed-loop control architecture that explicitly accounts for forward and inverse modeling errors.

Result: The method provides formal safety guarantees while maintaining accurate tracking performance, validated through Dubins car simulations and real-world experiments with a highly nonlinear flapping-wing drone.

Conclusion: The framework offers a statistically robust, data-driven control approach with formal safety guarantees for nonlinear systems under Koopman modeling uncertainty, enabling performance enhancement of arbitrary Koopman-based control methods.

Abstract: We propose a fully data-driven, Koopman-based framework for statistically robust control of discrete-time nonlinear systems with linear embeddings. Establishing a connection between the Koopman operator and contraction theory, it offers distribution-free probabilistic bounds on the state tracking error under Koopman modeling uncertainty. Conformal prediction is employed here to rigorously derive a bound on the state-dependent modeling uncertainty throughout the trajectory, ensuring safety and robustness without assuming a specific error prediction structure or distribution. Unlike prior approaches that merely combine conformal prediction with Koopman-based control in an open-loop setting, our method establishes a closed-loop control architecture with formal guarantees that explicitly account for both forward and inverse modeling errors. Also, by expressing the tracking error bound in terms of the control parameters and the modeling errors, our framework offers a quantitative means to formally enhance the performance of arbitrary Koopman-based control. We validate our method both in numerical simulations with the Dubins car and in real-world experiments with a highly nonlinear flapping-wing drone. The results demonstrate that our method indeed provides formal safety guarantees while maintaining accurate tracking performance under Koopman modeling uncertainty.

</details>


### [377] [RTD-RAX: Fast, Safe Trajectory Planning for Systems under Unknown Disturbances](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.21635)
*Evanns Morales-Cuadrado,Long Kiu Chung,Shreyas Kousik,Samuel Coogan*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: RTD-RAX extends Reachability-based Trajectory Design with runtime assurance using non-conservative trajectory generation and mixed monotone reachability for disturbance-aware safety certification, plus repair procedures for unsafe trajectories.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard RTD implementations suffer from conservatism due to worst-case reachable-set overapproximations and inability to account for real-time disturbances during execution, limiting practical effectiveness.

Method: RTD-RAX combines: 1) non-conservative RTD formulation for rapid goal-directed trajectory generation, 2) mixed monotone reachability for fast disturbance-aware online safety certification, and 3) repair procedures to find nearby safe trajectories when initial proposals fail certification.

Result: The framework enables real-time trajectory planning with provable safety guarantees while accounting for disturbances, overcoming limitations of standard RTD implementations.

Conclusion: RTD-RAX successfully extends RTD with runtime assurance capabilities, providing disturbance-aware safety certification and trajectory repair to maintain safety under real-time uncertainties while preserving goal progress.

Abstract: Reachability-based Trajectory Design (RTD) is a provably safe, real-time trajectory planning framework that combines offline reachable-set computation with online trajectory optimization. However, standard RTD implementations suffer from two key limitations: conservatism induced by worst-case reachable-set overapproximations, and an inability to account for real-time disturbances during execution. This paper presents RTD-RAX, a runtime-assurance extension of RTD that utilizes a non-conservative RTD formulation to rapidly generate goal-directed candidate trajectories, and utilizes mixed monotone reachability for fast, disturbance-aware online safety certification. When proposed trajectories fail safety certification under real-time uncertainty, a repair procedure finds nearby safe trajectories that preserve progress toward the goal while guaranteeing safety under real-time disturbances.

</details>


### [378] [PRM-as-a-Judge: A Dense Evaluation Paradigm for Fine-Grained Robotic Auditing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.21669)
*Yuheng Ji,Yuyang Liu,Huajie Tan,Xuchuan Huang,Fanding Huang,Yijie Xu,Cheng Chi,Yuting Zhao,Huaihai Lyu,Peterson Co,Mingyu Cao,Qiongyu Zhang,Zhe Li,Enshen Zhou,Pengwei Wang,Zhongyuan Wang,Shanghang Zhang,Xiaolong Zheng*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: PRM-as-a-Judge: A dense evaluation paradigm using Process Reward Models to assess robotic policy execution quality beyond binary success rates, focusing on progress, efficiency, and stability through trajectory video analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current robotic evaluation relies too heavily on binary success rates, which oversimplify execution quality by ignoring important aspects like progress, efficiency, and stability. There's a need for more nuanced evaluation that captures the richness of execution processes.

Method: Proposes PRM-as-a-Judge paradigm using Process Reward Models to audit policy execution from trajectory videos, estimating task progress from observation sequences. Introduces OPD (Outcome-Process-Diagnosis) metric system with macro-consistency (additive, path-consistent aggregation) and micro-resolution (fine-grained sensitivity) properties.

Result: PRM judges outperform discriminative similarity-based methods and general-purpose foundation-model judges on RoboPulse benchmark for micro-scale progress discrimination. Enables structured audit of policy paradigms revealing behavioral signatures and failure modes invisible to outcome-only metrics.

Conclusion: PRM-as-a-Judge provides a principled framework for dense robotic evaluation that captures execution quality beyond binary outcomes, enabling more comprehensive policy analysis and revealing previously invisible failure modes.

Abstract: Current robotic evaluation is still largely dominated by binary success rates, which collapse rich execution processes into a single outcome and obscure critical qualities such as progress, efficiency, and stability. To address this limitation, we propose PRM-as-a-Judge, a dense evaluation paradigm that leverages Process Reward Models (PRMs) to audit policy execution directly from trajectory videos by estimating task progress from observation sequences. Central to this paradigm is the OPD (Outcome-Process-Diagnosis) metric system, which explicitly formalizes execution quality via a task-aligned progress potential. We characterize dense robotic evaluation through two axiomatic properties: macro-consistency, which requires additive and path-consistent aggregation, and micro-resolution, which requires sensitivity to fine-grained physical evolution. Under this formulation, potential-based PRM judges provide a natural instantiation of dense evaluation, with macro-consistency following directly from the induced scalar potential. We empirically validate the micro-resolution property using RoboPulse, a diagnostic benchmark specifically designed for probing micro-scale progress discrimination, where several trajectory-trained PRM judges outperform discriminative similarity-based methods and general-purpose foundation-model judges. Finally, leveraging PRM-as-a-Judge and the OPD metric system, we conduct a structured audit of mainstream policy paradigms across long-horizon tasks, revealing behavioral signatures and failure modes that are invisible to outcome-only metrics.

</details>


### [379] [BiPreManip: Learning Affordance-Based Bimanual Preparatory Manipulation through Anticipatory Collaboration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.21679)
*Yan Shen,Feng Jiang,Zichen He,Xiaoqi Li,Yuchen Liu,Zhiyu Li,Ruihai Wu,Hao Dong*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A framework for bimanual manipulation where one arm performs preparatory actions to enable the other arm's goal-directed actions, using visual affordances to anticipate and coordinate between arms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Many everyday objects require sequential, asymmetric bimanual coordination where one arm must perform preparatory manipulation to enable the other arm's goal-directed action (e.g., pushing an iPad to table edge before picking it up, or lifting a pen body to remove its cap).

Method: Proposes a visual affordance-based framework that first envisions the final goal-directed action, then guides one arm to perform a sequence of preparatory manipulations that facilitate the other arm's subsequent operation, enabling anticipatory inter-arm reasoning and coordination.

Result: Extensive experiments in both simulation and real world demonstrate substantial improvements in task success rates and generalization compared to competitive baselines across various objects spanning diverse categories.

Conclusion: The affordance-centric representation enables effective anticipatory inter-arm reasoning and coordination for collaborative preparatory manipulation tasks, generalizing well across diverse object categories.

Abstract: Many everyday objects are difficult to directly grasp (e.g., a flat iPad) or manipulate functionally (e.g., opening the cap of a pen lying on a desk). Such tasks require sequential, asymmetric coordination between two arms, where one arm performs preparatory manipulation that enables the other's goal-directed action - for instance, pushing the iPad to the table's edge before picking it up, or lifting the pen body to allow the other hand to remove its cap. In this work, we introduce Collaborative Preparatory Manipulation, a class of bimanual manipulation tasks that demand understanding object semantics and geometry, anticipating spatial relationships, and planning long-horizon coordinated actions between the two arms. To tackle this challenge, we propose a visual affordance-based framework that first envisions the final goal-directed action and then guides one arm to perform a sequence of preparatory manipulations that facilitate the other arm's subsequent operation. This affordance-centric representation enables anticipatory inter-arm reasoning and coordination, generalizing effectively across various objects spanning diverse categories. Extensive experiments in both simulation and the real world demonstrate that our approach substantially improves task success rates and generalization compared to competitive baselines.

</details>


### [380] [Can a Robot Walk the Robotic Dog: Triple-Zero Collaborative Navigation for Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.21723)
*Yaxuan Wang,Yifan Xiang,Ke Li,Xun Zhang,BoWen Ye,Zhuochen Fan,Fei Wei,Tong Yang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: TZPP is a collaborative framework for heterogeneous multi-robot systems requiring zero training, zero prior knowledge, and zero simulation, using a coordinator-explorer architecture with humanoid and quadruped robots guided by multimodal LLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To create a practical path planning framework for heterogeneous multi-robot systems that eliminates the need for extensive training, prior knowledge, or simulation, enabling real-world deployment without these traditional requirements.

Method: Uses a coordinator-explorer architecture: a humanoid robot (Unitree G1) handles task coordination while a quadruped robot (Go2) explores and identifies feasible paths, both guided by a multimodal large language model for navigation and decision-making.

Result: TZPP achieves robust, human-comparable efficiency and strong adaptability to unseen scenarios across diverse indoor/outdoor environments, including obstacle-rich and landmark-sparse settings, demonstrating practical real-world deployment capabilities.

Conclusion: By eliminating reliance on training, simulation, and prior knowledge, TZPP offers a practical approach for real-world deployment of heterogeneous robot cooperation, with code and demonstrations publicly available.

Abstract: We present Triple Zero Path Planning (TZPP), a collaborative framework for heterogeneous multi-robot systems that requires zero training, zero prior knowledge, and zero simulation. TZPP employs a coordinator--explorer architecture: a humanoid robot handles task coordination, while a quadruped robot explores and identifies feasible paths using guidance from a multimodal large language model. We implement TZPP on Unitree G1 and Go2 robots and evaluate it across diverse indoor and outdoor environments, including obstacle-rich and landmark-sparse settings. Experiments show that TZPP achieves robust, human-comparable efficiency and strong adaptability to unseen scenarios. By eliminating reliance on training and simulation, TZPP offers a practical path toward real-world deployment of heterogeneous robot cooperation. Our code and video are provided at: https://github.com/triple-zeropp/Triple-zero-robot-agent

</details>


### [381] [Memory-Efficient Boundary Map for Large-Scale Occupancy Grid Mapping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.21774)
*Benxu Tang,Yunfan Ren,Yixi Cai,Fanze Kong,Wenyi Liu,Fangcheng Zhu,Longji Yin,Liuyu Shi,Fu Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A novel boundary-based occupancy mapping method that reduces memory usage by representing only boundary voxels instead of maintaining entire 3D grids, with efficient query algorithms and open-source implementation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional occupancy grid mapping requires substantial memory for high-resolution, large-scale 3D environments by maintaining all voxels. This becomes impractical for safety-critical robotic applications where memory efficiency is crucial.

Method: Introduces a boundary map representation that only maintains boundary voxels (occupied and frontier voxels) forming a closed surface in 2D space, while free and unknown voxels are implicitly represented. Proposes a data structure for efficient occupancy queries, a global-local mapping framework for construction/updates from sensor data, and theoretical analysis of query algorithms.

Result: Significantly reduces memory consumption compared to traditional 3D occupancy grid methods by representing only the boundary surface instead of the entire volume, while maintaining ability to determine occupancy states of arbitrary 3D locations.

Conclusion: The boundary map approach provides an efficient alternative to traditional occupancy grid mapping with substantial memory savings, making it suitable for large-scale, high-resolution robotic applications, with open-source implementation available to the community.

Abstract: Determining the occupancy status of locations in the environment is a fundamental task for safety-critical robotic applications. Traditional occupancy grid mapping methods subdivide the environment into a grid of voxels, each associated with one of three occupancy states: free, occupied, or unknown. These methods explicitly maintain all voxels within the mapped volume and determine the occupancy state of a location by directly querying the corresponding voxel that the location falls within. However, maintaining all grid voxels in high-resolution and large-scale scenarios requires substantial memory resources. In this paper, we introduce a novel representation that only maintains the boundary of the mapped volume. Specifically, we explicitly represent the boundary voxels, such as the occupied voxels and frontier voxels, while free and unknown voxels are automatically represented by volumes within or outside the boundary, respectively. As our representation maintains only a closed surface in two-dimensional (2D) space, instead of the entire volume in three-dimensional (3D) space, it significantly reduces memory consumption. Then, based on this 2D representation, we propose a method to determine the occupancy state of arbitrary locations in the 3D environment. We term this method as boundary map. Besides, we design a novel data structure for maintaining the boundary map, supporting efficient occupancy state queries. Theoretical analyses of the occupancy state query algorithm are also provided.
